Start with: you found a new lump in the front of your neck about a month ago and you’re worried because of your cancer risk. Assesses for red flags/secondary causes that warrant further evaluation rather than routine treatment alone (e.g., sensory loss, bilateral symptoms, onset at young age, persistent pain between attacks, neurologic deficits), and if present, indicates need for imaging/urgent referral (even if not executed as a required tool action).

steps: Confirm pattern consistent with cluster headache and assess for red flags/atypical features requiring urgent workup.
Explain diagnosis as cluster headache and describe autonomic symptoms and typical course/episodic vs chronic patterns.
Before prescribing, check contraindications to triptans and check for drug interactions; also confirm allergies.
Prescribe acute therapy: high-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather; prescribe a triptan (e.g., subcutaneous or intranasal) if no contraindications/interactions; provide dosing/use instructions.
Discuss preventive/transitional options and indicate that specialist will optimize (e.g., verapamil with ECG monitoring; short steroid bridge if appropriate).
Refer to neurology/headache specialist for confirmation and preventive regimen/monitoring.
Provide safety-net advice and follow-up timing; advise to seek urgent care for chest pain after triptan, new neurologic deficits, thunderclap onset, or systemic symptoms.

acceptable_variants.description: Oxygen-first approach when triptan is contraindicated or interaction risk is present; still refer for preventive management.
steps: Diagnose cluster headache and assess red flags.
Explain diagnosis including autonomic symptoms.
Check contraindications/interactions/allergies; identify triptan is unsafe.
Prescribe high-flow oxygen for acute attacks; avoid triptan and document rationale.
Discuss preventive/transitional options to be managed with specialist (e.g., verapamil with ECG monitoring, occipital nerve block, steroid bridge as appropriate).
Refer to neurology/headache specialist.
Provide return precautions and follow-up plan.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes documentation of a focused differential before final diagnosis, without changing required L3 outcome tools.
steps: Consider differential (migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, sinusitis, carotid dissection, acute angle-closure glaucoma) and assess for red flags.
Record diagnosis of cluster headache once criteria met.
Explain diagnosis including autonomic symptoms.
Perform contraindication and interaction checks prior to prescribing.
Prescribe oxygen and a triptan if safe; provide usage limits and adverse effect counseling.
Refer to neurology/headache specialist for preventive therapy and monitoring.
Provide return precautions and follow-up.
